skill.name: secure-linux-web-hosting
skill.description: Use when setting up, hardening, or reviewing a cloud server for self-hosting, including DNS, SSH, firewalls, Nginx, static-site hosting, reverse-proxying an app, HTTPS with Let's Encrypt or ACME clients, safe HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects, or optional post-launch network tuning such as BBR.
skill.summary: Guide secure Linux web hosting setup with distro-aware SSH, firewall, Nginx, DNS, and TLS sequencing.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many command, sudo, URL, IP, and weak-crypto patterns, but the reviewed files are markdown guidance and reference material rather than executable code. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, malware behavior, or hidden execution path was found. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it guides privileged Linux hosting, firewall, SSH, Nginx, TLS, and network validation actions.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Server Administration Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill gives operational guidance for SSH hardening, firewall posture, package installation, Nginx configuration, TLS issuance, service validation, and optional kernel or network tuning. These are legitimate hosting tasks, but mistakes can lock out administrators, expose services, or break a live site.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly describe privileged hosting workflows and safety gates. The risk is operational misuse, not malicious behavior or hidden execution.

medium_findings.title: Network Exposure and Diagnostic Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill references firewall openings, public web ports, DNS checks, HTTP checks, socket inspection, and TLS validation. These checks are expected for web hosting, but they should be applied only to authorized systems and reviewed before changing live exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain normal hosting validation and exposure checks. The context confirms legitimate administration, with risk limited to careless use.

low_findings.title: Static Command Pattern Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static shell-execution findings are caused by markdown inline commands and configuration examples. No executable script, command wrapper, or user-input command construction was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns appear inside markdown examples and validation lists. I found no runtime code path that would execute these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Official Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to distro, Nginx, Let's Encrypt, Certbot, acme.sh, and source article documentation. These links do not collect data or direct requests to suspicious endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references in markdown. No evidence of network exfiltration, credential handling, or automated request behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts are false positives from text such as SSH, HTTPS, TLS, and ACME guidance. No code implements weak algorithms or recommends obsolete cryptographic settings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed content discusses TLS client selection and safe sequencing. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithm implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Facing Administrative Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill may lead an assistant to provide commands for package managers, firewalls, system services, Nginx, DNS, socket checks, and certificate tooling. This is expected for the skill domain, but commands should be verified against official documentation before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill intentionally routes users toward administrative command guidance. Its own instructions require verification, which lowers malicious-risk confidence but not operational-risk confidence.

content.value_statement: Cloud server setup often fails because SSH, firewall, DNS, Nginx, and TLS steps happen in the wrong order. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through verified, distro-aware hosting and hardening workflows.
actual_capabilities: Routes hosting advice by Linux distribution family, package manager, firewall tooling, and service layout.
Separates static Nginx hosting from reverse proxy hosting for app backends.
Defines safe sequencing for SSH hardening, firewall changes, HTTP setup, HTTPS issuance, and redirects.
Prompts verification against official distro, Nginx, Certbot, Let's Encrypt, and acme.sh documentation.
Provides validation checkpoints for DNS, Nginx syntax, service health, HTTP responses, and TLS behavior.
Keeps optional BBR and kernel tuning after the core hosting path is stable.
limitations: It does not execute server changes or verify a live host by itself.
It does not replace distro documentation, provider documentation, or a production SRE review.
It does not cover Kubernetes, PaaS deployment, Windows hosting, or large multi-tenant architecture.
It requires user-provided details about the distro, DNS state, access model, and hosting goal.

use_cases.title: Launch a Small Static Site
use_cases.description: Plan a secure order for DNS, Nginx static files, firewall rules, HTTPS, and redirect validation.

use_cases.title: Proxy a Private App
use_cases.description: Keep an application on loopback while Nginx handles public HTTP, HTTPS, headers, and validation.

use_cases.title: Review a Cloud Server
use_cases.description: Check SSH hardening, open ports, certificate readiness, Nginx branch choice, and risky sequencing.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Hosting Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a secure Linux web hosting setup. Ask for my distro, domain status, SSH access, firewall state, and whether I need static hosting or a reverse proxy.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Static Nginx Hosting
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through a safe static-site Nginx setup. Separate local and server actions, include validation after each risky step, and verify distro-specific details first.

prompt_templates.title: Review Reverse Proxy Safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my plan to put an app behind Nginx. Check whether the app stays on loopback, which ports are exposed, and what I should validate before HTTPS.

prompt_templates.title: Audit TLS and Hardening Sequence
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my SSH, firewall, Nginx, DNS, and TLS sequence for lockout or exposure risks. Prioritize the next safe verification step before any change.

output_examples.input: I have an Ubuntu server and want to host a static site with HTTPS.
output: A phase-based setup plan that starts with distro and DNS verification.
Separate local and server steps for access, firewall, Nginx, HTTP, TLS, and redirect validation.
Warnings to avoid SSH lockout and premature HTTPS redirects.

output_examples.input: My app runs on port 3000 and I want Nginx in front of it.
output: A reverse-proxy checklist that keeps the app bound to loopback.
Validation steps for local app response, Nginx health, public HTTP, certificate issuance, and HTTPS.
Failure branches for service, firewall, DNS, and policy-control issues.

best_practices: Keep a second SSH session or rescue path before tightening authentication.
Verify distro-specific package, service, firewall, and ACME instructions before giving commands.
Validate HTTP and HTTPS behavior before enabling permanent redirects or optional tuning.
anti_patterns: Do not apply old Debian commands to an unknown Linux distribution.
Do not expose app backend ports publicly when Nginx should front the app.
Do not start BBR or kernel tuning before secure hosting already works.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands on my server?
faq.answer: No. It guides planning and review. You or your AI tool decide which commands to run.

faq.question: Which Linux distributions does it support?
faq.answer: It routes by distro family and asks the assistant to verify current official documentation before detailed commands.

faq.question: Can it help with Let's Encrypt certificates?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers ACME preconditions, client choice, issuance readiness, renewal posture, HTTPS validation, and redirect timing.

faq.question: Can it configure a reverse proxy?
faq.answer: Yes. It separates reverse-proxy guidance from static hosting and emphasizes loopback app binding.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production architecture reviews?
faq.answer: It is useful for small-host hardening, but broad multi-tenant production designs need a deeper SRE review.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level medium?
faq.answer: The content is not malicious, but it guides privileged server, firewall, SSH, TLS, and network exposure decisions.
